title: The Rootstock Guide
subtitle: Phylloxera, grafting, and the roots beneath the vine

overview.title: The Foundation of Modern Viticulture
overview.description: Every wine grape grown in the Old World, and most in the New, rests on borrowed roots. The phylloxera crisis of the 1860s devastated European vineyards, destroying 2.5 million hectares and reshaping viticulture forever. The solution came from an unlikely source: American wild vine species. Today, rootstock selection is as crucial as clone selection, determining vine vigor, stress tolerance, and ultimately, wine quality.

history.title: The Phylloxera Crisis
history.description: How a microscopic aphid brought European viticulture to its knees and forced a revolutionary solution.

timeline.event: First signs of vine decline observed in southern Rhone Valley near Arles. Vines inexplicably dying.
timeline.significance: The beginning of the greatest agricultural disaster in European history

timeline.event: Jules-Emile Planchon identifies the culprit: a tiny aphid feeding on vine roots, which he names Phylloxera vastatrix (the devastator).
timeline.significance: Understanding the enemy was the first step toward fighting it

timeline.event: Charles Valentine Riley, American entomologist, independently studies the pest and begins correspondence with Planchon.
timeline.significance: The Franco-American collaboration that would save European wine begins

timeline.event: Phylloxera spreads across France at 20-30 km per year. Bordeaux, Burgundy, Champagne successively devastated.
Leo Laliman proposes grafting European vines onto American rootstocks. Controversial idea initially rejected by many.
timeline.significance: The eventual solution is proposed but not yet accepted

timeline.event: International Phylloxera Congress in Bordeaux. Grafting gains acceptance as only viable solution.
Pierre Viala travels to Texas on French government mission, discovers V. berlandieri growing in chalk soils similar to Champagne and Cognac.
timeline.significance: The missing piece: a rootstock for calcareous soils

timeline.event: Systematic replanting begins across Europe. By century's end, most European vineyards are grafted.
2.5 million hectares destroyed. France alone lost 40% of vineyard area permanently.

impact.economic: Billions lost in vineyard value, wine production collapsed, rural exodus from wine regions
impact.social: Mass emigration from wine regions, French vignerons fled to Algeria, Argentina, Chile, establishing New World wine industries
impact.viticultural: Forced modernization: replanting allowed rationalization of vineyard layouts, introduction of new varieties, abandonment of marginal sites

pioneers.name: Jules-Emile Planchon
pioneers.contribution: Identified phylloxera as the cause, advocated for American rootstocks

pioneers.name: Charles Valentine Riley
pioneers.contribution: Proved phylloxera originated in America, championed grafting solution

pioneers.name: Pierre Viala
pioneers.contribution: Texas expedition discovering V. berlandieri for calcareous soils

pioneers.name: Leo Laliman
pioneers.contribution: First to propose grafting solution in France (1869)

pioneers.name: Alexis Millardet
pioneers.contribution: Developed Bordeaux mixture for mildew, also worked on rootstock hybridization

solution.grafting_discovery: American vine species co-evolved with phylloxera over millions of years, developing resistance. While the aphid could still feed on their roots, the damage was not fatal. Grafting European V. vinifera scions onto American rootstocks combined the best of both worlds: European fruit quality on resistant American roots.

own_rooted_today.description: Some regions escaped phylloxera entirely due to geographic isolation or soil conditions inhospitable to the pest.

phylloxera_free_regions.reason: Natural barriers: Andes Mountains, Atacama Desert, Pacific Ocean, and Antarctic ice isolate the country
phylloxera_free_regions.notes: Home to some of the oldest ungrafted Carmenere and Pais vines in the world

phylloxera_free_regions.reason: Volcanic pumice soil is inhospitable to phylloxera
phylloxera_free_regions.notes: Assyrtiko vines trained in basket shapes (kouloura) can be 200+ years old
Ancient Listan Negro vines, some pre-phylloxera

phylloxera_free_regions.reason: Geographic isolation and strict quarantine. Phylloxera present but contained
phylloxera_free_regions.notes: Barossa Valley maintains phylloxera-free status with ancient Shiraz vines
Much of Columbia Valley remains phylloxera-free
Ramisco vines on own roots, protected by sand

francs_de_pied_movement.description: A movement of winemakers growing vines on their own roots in phylloxera-prone areas, seeking UNESCO recognition
francs_de_pied_movement.proponents: Some Loire Valley, Alsace, and Swiss producers
francs_de_pied_movement.argument: Own-rooted vines produce wines with more mineral expression and direct connection to terroir
francs_de_pied_movement.risk: High - vines typically succumb within 15-20 years

species.title: American Vine Species
species.description: Three American species form the foundation of modern rootstocks. Each offers different characteristics, and most commercial rootstocks are hybrids combining their traits.

items.name: Vitis riparia
items.native_range: Northern US and Canada, along rivers and streams
items.rootstock_contribution: Provides phylloxera resistance, early ripening, and devigorating effect. Often crossed with V. rupestris for riparia x rupestris hybrids like 3309C.

items.name: Vitis rupestris
items.native_range: Central and Southern US, rocky/sandy soils
items.limestone_tolerance: Low to moderate (up to 14% active lime)
characteristics: Deep rooting, accesses water in dry conditions
Better drought tolerance than V. riparia
items.rootstock_contribution: Contributes vigor, drought tolerance, and deep rooting. Often crossed with V. riparia (3309C, 101-14) or V. berlandieri (110R, 140Ru).
items.soil_preference: Well-drained, rocky, sandy, tolerates poor soils

items.name: Vitis berlandieri
items.native_range: Texas and Northern Mexico, chalk hills
characteristics: Late budbreak, protects from spring frost
Very difficult to root from cuttings
Discovered by Pierre Viala in Texas (1887)
Key to Champagne and Cognac replanting
items.rootstock_contribution: Essential for calcareous soils. Provides limestone tolerance and drought resistance. Always used in crosses because pure V. berlandieri roots poorly.

viala_discovery.context: French government sent Pierre Viala to Texas to find rootstocks for chalk soils after existing American species failed in Champagne and Cognac
viala_discovery.significance: Without V. berlandieri, Champagne and Cognac could not have been replanted

rootstocks.title: Rootstock Varieties
rootstocks.description: Commercial rootstocks are selected hybrids, bred for specific combinations of resistance, vigor, and soil adaptation. Each has strengths and weaknesses that must match site conditions.

varieties.name: SO4
characteristics: One of the most widely planted rootstocks globally
Advances ripening by a few days
High potassium uptake can reduce color/acidity
regional_use: Germany: Standard rootstock, especially for Riesling
Northern Italy: Widely used for Pinot Grigio, Chardonnay
Oregon: Popular for Pinot Noir in cooler sites

varieties.name: 3309C
characteristics: Bordeaux #1 rootstock for quality red wines
Devigorating effect prized for premium vineyards
Sensitive to drought - needs adequate water
Excellent for Merlot and Cabernet Sauvignon
regional_use: Bordeaux: Dominant rootstock in classified growths
New Zealand: Popular for Pinot Noir
Burgundy: Used in some premier cru sites

characteristics: Very sensitive to drought - needs irrigation or wet climate
Good for controlling vigor in fertile soils
Popular in New Zealand and Oregon
regional_use: New Zealand: Very popular, suits wet climate

varieties.name: 110R
characteristics: Excellent drought tolerance with very deep rooting
Lower potassium uptake - helps maintain acidity
regional_use: Australia: Standard for warm, dry regions
California: Popular in Napa, Paso Robles
Spain: Widely used in La Mancha, Rioja

varieties.name: 140Ru
characteristics: Very vigorous - can be excessive in fertile soils
regional_use: Southern Spain: Sherry region, La Mancha

varieties.name: 1103P
characteristics: Best salt tolerance of commercial rootstocks
Low potassium uptake - maintains acidity and color
Popular in coastal and irrigated regions
regional_use: California: Central Valley, where salinity is issue
Middle East: Israel, where soil salinity is common

varieties.name: 420A
characteristics: Devigorating effect prized for premium Bordeaux
Low-K helps maintain color and acidity
Used in many classified Bordeaux estates

varieties.name: Riparia Gloire
characteristics: Promotes early ripening and small berries
Prized in Burgundy for premium Pinot Noir
Very sensitive to stress - needs ideal conditions
regional_use: Burgundy: Grand Cru and Premier Cru Pinot Noir
Bordeaux: Some top estates on non-calcareous soils

varieties.name: 41B
characteristics: Highest limestone tolerance of traditional rootstocks
Contains V. vinifera parent - provides compatibility but reduces phylloxera resistance
Sensitive to iron chlorosis despite lime tolerance
Being superseded by Fercal in some areas
regional_use: Champagne: Dominant, up to 80-90% of plantings
Loire Valley: Chalk soils of Vouvray, Saumur
varieties.caution: V. vinifera parentage means phylloxera resistance is not absolute. Monitor for biotype pressure.

varieties.name: 5BB
characteristics: Very high potassium uptake - reduces color and acidity

varieties.name: Schwarzmann
characteristics: Best nematode resistance among traditional European rootstocks
Used where nematodes are present but soil not calcareous
Popular in some New World regions

varieties.name: 161-49C
characteristics: Good limestone tolerance without excessive vigor

parentage.composition: V. berlandieri x (V. vinifera x V. berlandieri)

varieties.name: AXR1
characteristics: V. vinifera parentage provided insufficient phylloxera resistance
Succumbed to Phylloxera Biotype B beginning 1983
Required $1+ billion in replanting across Napa/Sonoma
Lesson: Never use rootstock with V. vinifera parent in phylloxera areas

historical_failure.description: The AXR1 disaster is viticulture's greatest cautionary tale

timeline.event: UC Davis recommends AXR1 for easy propagation and good graft compatibility
70% of Napa Valley planted to AXR1
First vines showing decline in Napa. Biotype B phylloxera identified
Mass replanting begins across California. Billions in losses

historical_failure.lesson: The V. vinifera parent (Aramon) provided easy rooting but fatal susceptibility. Rootstocks with any V. vinifera parentage must be monitored carefully.

varieties.name: Freedom
characteristics: Often used in table grape and raisin production
Higher vigor can dilute wine quality
regional_use: Australia: Some areas with nematode pressure

varieties.name: Harmony
best_for: Where less vigor than Freedom needed
characteristics: Similar to Freedom but slightly less vigorous
regional_use: Warm climate regions with nematode issues

selection.title: Rootstock Selection Criteria
selection.description: Choosing the right rootstock requires matching vine needs to site conditions. No perfect rootstock exists - each represents trade-offs between vigor, resistance, and adaptation.

factors.description: Critical for calcareous soils. Vines on wrong rootstock develop iron chlorosis - yellow leaves, poor growth.
Deep rooting and water efficiency. Critical for non-irrigated sites and climate change adaptation.
Lower vigor = smaller canopy, smaller berries, better quality. Essential for premium production.
Critical for sandy soils and replant situations. Traditional European rootstocks generally poor.
Important for irrigated regions and coastal areas with saline groundwater.
High-K rootstocks increase grape pH and reduce color. Low-K preferred for red wine quality.

decision_matrix.description: Match site conditions to rootstock selection

regions.title: Regional Rootstock Preferences
regions.description: Each wine region has developed preferences based on soil type, climate, and historical experience. These patterns reflect decades of trial and observation.

regions.name: Champagne

primary_rootstocks.name: 41B
primary_rootstocks.reason: Highest limestone tolerance, proven over century

secondary_rootstocks.name: Fercal
secondary_rootstocks.reason: Better chlorosis resistance, replacing 41B in new plantings

regions.soil_notes: Pure chalk subsoils with high active lime. Iron chlorosis is the primary concern.
regions.climate_notes: Cool continental, adequate moisture, spring frost risk
regions.historical_context: After phylloxera, Champagne could not replant until V. berlandieri was discovered in Texas and crossed to create 41B.
regions.modern_trends: Fercal gaining ground for better overall performance. Climate change reducing frost pressure.
unique_factors: Up to 40% active limestone in subsoils
Vignerons refer to rootstock by shorthand: 'quarante-et-un B'
Deep chalk forces deep rooting, contributing to minerality

regions.name: Burgundy
regions.key_challenge: Balancing limestone tolerance with quality (low vigor)

primary_rootstocks.name: 3309C
primary_rootstocks.reason: Good compromise of vigor and adaptation

regions.soil_notes: Variable: marl, limestone, clay over limestone. Active lime 5-25% depending on site.
regions.climate_notes: Cool continental, variable rainfall, spring frost risk
regions.historical_context: Riparia Gloire long favored for Grand Cru quality, but limited by limestone sensitivity.
regions.modern_trends: Climate change increasing drought - some shift toward more tolerant rootstocks. Site-specific selection emphasized.
unique_factors: Quality hierarchy influences rootstock choice
Grands Crus often on Riparia Gloire where soil permits
Village wines more likely on SO4 for practicality

regions.name: Bordeaux
regions.key_challenge: Diverse soils from gravel to clay to limestone

primary_rootstocks.name: 3309C
primary_rootstocks.reason: Quality focus, low vigor, proven track record

regions.soil_notes: Left Bank: gravel over clay. Right Bank: clay, limestone, clay over limestone.
regions.historical_context: 3309C became dominant after WWII replanting. Quality correlation established.
regions.modern_trends: Saint-Emilion plateau needs Fercal or 41B. Climate change less impactful due to maritime influence.
unique_factors: Classified growths heavily favor 3309C
Left/Right Bank divide in rootstock preferences
Humidity makes drought tolerance less critical

regions.name: Cognac
regions.soil_notes: Grande Champagne, Petite Champagne: pure chalk. Borderies: clay-limestone. Fins Bois: varied.
regions.historical_context: Same crisis as Champagne - needed V. berlandieri discovery for replanting.
unique_factors: Ugni Blanc (Trebbiano) is primary variety
High yield focus (distillation)
Chalk soils drive 41B dominance

regions.name: Napa Valley
regions.key_challenge: Recovery from AXR1 failure, diverse conditions
regions.soil_notes: Extremely diverse: volcanic, alluvial, clay, gravel, serpentine. Generally acidic to neutral.
regions.climate_notes: Mediterranean, dry summers, frost risk varies by location
regions.historical_context: AXR1 disaster (1983-2000) forced complete replanting. Lesson: no V. vinifera rootstocks.

axr1_history.lesson: Never use rootstock with V. vinifera parentage

regions.modern_trends: Climate change driving toward drought-tolerant rootstocks. 110R and 1103P gaining.
unique_factors: Extreme diversity of soil and mesoclimate
Strong Bordeaux influence in variety and rootstock
Recent memory of AXR1 informs choices

regions.name: Australia
regions.soil_notes: Ancient, nutrient-poor soils. Often sandy or clay. Salinity from irrigation.
regions.climate_notes: Hot, dry. Drought is perennial challenge.

phylloxera_status.description: Phylloxera present in Victoria but contained
phylloxera_status.quarantine: Strict quarantine protocols, boot washing, equipment cleaning

regions.own_rooted_significance: Barossa old vine Shiraz on own roots (some 150+ years). Considered cultural heritage.
regions.modern_trends: Drought and salinity driving rootstock adoption even in phylloxera-free zones. Insurance against future spread.
unique_factors: Phylloxera-free zones maintain ungrafted vines
Old vine ungrafted Shiraz prized
Salt tolerance increasingly important

regions.name: Chile
regions.soil_notes: Diverse: alluvial, volcanic, granite, clay. Generally non-calcareous.
regions.climate_notes: Mediterranean, dry summers, Andes snowmelt irrigation

phylloxera_free_reason.significance: Home to some of the world's oldest Carmenere vines

regions.own_rooted_significance: Chile maintains genetic diversity unavailable in grafted regions. Ancient Pais, pre-phylloxera Carmenere.
regions.modern_trends: Some rootstock adoption for replant situations, nematodes, drought stress management. But 85%+ remains ungrafted.
unique_factors: Pre-phylloxera genetic material preserved
Old Carmenere, Pais vines on own roots
Rootstock use increasing slowly for management reasons

modern.title: Modern Developments
modern.description: Climate change and advancing science are reshaping rootstock research. New breeding programs target drought tolerance, virus resistance, and adaptation to unprecedented conditions.

breeding_programs.focus: Drought tolerance, nematode resistance, phylloxera resistance
breeding_programs.notable_work: GRN (Grapevine Rootstock Network) consortium, mapping rootstock genetics
breeding_programs.recent_releases: Research on M-series adaptations for California

breeding_programs.focus: Limestone tolerance, climate adaptation, virus tolerance
Drought, salt tolerance, stacked resistance traits
breeding_programs.notable_work: Breeding for Australian conditions: heat, drought, salinity
breeding_programs.recent_releases: Focus on combining multiple stress resistances

breeding_programs.notable_work: M-series rootstocks (M1, M2, M3, M4) for drought and limestone
breeding_programs.recent_releases: M4 showing promise for drought + moderate limestone

challenges: Earlier budburst exposing vines to late frost
rootstock_responses: Breeding for deeper rooting and water-use efficiency
Shift from vigor control to stress tolerance priorities
Rootstocks that delay ripening may help with sugar accumulation
Salt-tolerant rootstocks for regions with increasing irrigation
regional_shifts: Burgundy: Some movement away from Riparia Gloire toward SO4, 110R
Bordeaux: 110R and 1103P increasing for drought years
California: Drought tolerance now primary criterion for new plantings

new_rootstocks.name: Fercal
new_rootstocks.key_trait: Superseding 41B for extreme limestone with better chlorosis resistance

future_directions.description: Marker-assisted selection speeds development. Genomic prediction identifies promising crosses before field trials.
Breeding for multiple resistances simultaneously: drought + nematode + phylloxera + limestone tolerance.
Some rootstocks affect scion virus expression. Breeding for tolerance to leafroll and red blotch.
Interest in rootstocks that support soil health, mycorrhizal associations.
Soil mapping + rootstock databases = site-specific recommendations. Moving beyond regional generalizations.
